name: Lucretia
description: Lucretia was a noblewoman in ancient Rome, renowned for her beauty and virtue. Her tragic story is pivotal in the transition from the Roman Kingdom to the Roman Republic. She was the wife of Lucius Tarquinius Collatinus, and her rape by Sextus Tarquinius, the son of the tyrannical King Lucius Tarquinius Superbus, became the catalyst for the overthrow of the monarchy. After the assault, Lucretia revealed the crime to her husband, father, and other noblemen, demanding they avenge her honor. She then took her own life, choosing death over living with the shame of the violation. This act of self-sacrifice ignited a rebellion led by Lucius Junius Brutus, who rallied the Roman people against the Tarquin kings. The monarchy was abolished, and the Roman Republic was established, with Brutus and Collatinus becoming the first consuls. Lucretia's story symbolizes the Roman ideals of virtue, honor, and resistance to tyranny. Her death is a powerful symbol of the Roman people's commitment to freedom and justice, and she became a legendary figure whose name was invoked as a symbol of republican ideals throughout Roman history and beyond. She represents the strength and moral fiber that the Romans valued, and her sacrifice inspired generations to fight for liberty.
subtitle: Virtue, Sacrifice, and the Republic.

keyMyths.title: The Rape of Lucretia
keyMyths.description: Sextus Tarquinius, inflamed by Lucretia's beauty and virtue, threatened her and raped her. Lucretia, deeply shamed, revealed the crime and then committed suicide.

keyMyths.title: The Oath of Brutus
keyMyths.description: After Lucretia's death, Lucius Junius Brutus vowed to avenge her and overthrow the Tarquin kings. This oath sparked the rebellion that led to the establishment of the Roman Republic.

keyMyths.title: Lucretia's Legacy
keyMyths.description: Lucretia's sacrifice became a powerful symbol of Roman virtue and resistance to tyranny. She was remembered as a heroine who inspired the Roman people to fight for their freedom.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: Livy's history of Rome provides the most famous account of Lucretia's story.
sources.text: Livy's history of Rome provides the most famous account of Lucretia's story.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Ovid recounts the story of Lucretia in his poem about the Roman calendar.
sources.text: Ovid recounts the story of Lucretia in his poem about the Roman calendar.

sources.author: Dionysius of Halicarnassus
sources.description: Another ancient source that discusses the events surrounding Lucretia's death and the overthrow of the monarchy.
sources.text: Another ancient source that discusses the events surrounding Lucretia's death and the overthrow of the monarchy.
